coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Ta2O5 by introducing graphene quantum dot with excellent photocatalytic performance for degradation of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Tao, Wen
Xiulan, Sun
Yirui, Shen
Zaijun, Li
Yongqiang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Wide band gap, weak adsorption ability, poor electronic conductivity and insufficient charge transportation limit the applications of Ta2O5 in photodegradation of antibiotics. The paper reports synthesis of Ta2O5 photocatalyst by introducing glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (GGQD). The introduction of GGQD effectively prevents the hydrolysis of Ta(V) and finally leads to the formation of small Ta2O5 nanorods. The modification of graphene sheets on the Ta2O5 surface enhances the adsorption towards tetracycline. The presence of Ta4+ self-doped oxygen vacancy narrows the band gap of Ta2O5, optimizes the band position and creates new defect energy level. These improve the light absorption capacity to visible light and promotes the photogenerated charge transfer. The graphene as electron trap suppresses the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. The graphene as light sensitizer expands the absorption to visible light. The synergy of these multiple structure engineering realizes an excellent photocatalytic performance of Ta2O5 for solar-light driven tetracycline photodegradation. The photodegradation efficiency of 60 min solar-light irradiation is 6.6 times higher, apparent kinetic constant is 19.3 times higher and photocurrent density is 13.4 times over Ta2O5. The study also paves an avenue for construction of metal nanomaterials with desirable photoelectric properties in catalysis, energy storage and conversion and sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ta2O5 composite
Functional graphene quantum dot
Photocatalyst
Antibiotics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for synthesis of GGQD and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 (A), SEM (B), TEM (C), enlarged TEM (D), HRTEM images (E)and EDS elemental mappings of Ta (F), O (G), N (H), C (I) and merged elements (J) of GGQD-Ta2O5-6.
XRD patterns (A), Raman spectra (B), FTIR spectrum (C) and EPR spectra of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5.
High resolution XPS spectra of Ta 4 f (A), O 1 s (B), C 1 s (C) and N 1 s (D) for GGQD-Ta2O5-6.

UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (A) of GGQD, GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6, GGQD-Ta2O5-8 and Ta2O5, the plot (B) of the transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. the energy of light for GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6, GGQD-Ta2O5-8 and Ta2O5, the Mott-Schottky plots (C) for GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5 in 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution, Nyquist plots (D) of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5, photoluminescence spectra (E) of Ta2O5, GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and GGQD-Ta2O5-8 and linear sweep voltammogram plots (F) of Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 in 300 W xenon lamp illumination.

Solar-light driven photodegradation efficiencies of tetracycline with different photocatalysts (A) and corresponding first-order kinetic curves (B), and visible light driven photodegradation efficiencies (C) of tetracycline with different photocatalysts, first-order kinetic curves (D), and k values (E) under visible light radiation, and k values (F) under solar-light radiation and visible light radiation.

Soar-light driven photodegradation efficiencies of tetracycline with GGQD-Ta2O5-6 using different concentration of tetracycline (A) and different medium pH (B), photodegradation curves of tetracycline (C), and degradation efficiencies (D) of 60 min solar-light.

Solar-light driven photodegradation rates of tetracycline with GGQD-Ta2O5-6 in the absence and the presence of different quencher, and ESR spectra of radical adducts of DMPO-•OH (B), DMPO-•O2 - (C) and TEMP-1O2 in dark and visible light irradiation (D).
Energy band structures of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5 and transfer mechanism of photogenerated carriers in photocatalytic process with GGQD-Ta2O5-6 under solar-light irradiation.
Comparison of different photocatalysts for photodegradation of tetracycline.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Ta2O5 by introducing graphene quantum dot with excellent photocatalytic performance for degradation of tetracycline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wide band gap, weak adsorption ability, poor electronic conductivity and insufficient charge transportation limit the applications of Ta2O5 in photodegradation of antibiotics. The paper reports synthesis of Ta2O5 photocatalyst by introducing glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (GGQD). The introduction of GGQD effectively prevents the hydrolysis of Ta(V) and finally leads to the formation of small Ta2O5 nanorods. The modification of graphene sheets on the Ta2O5 surface enhances the adsorption towards tetracycline. The presence of Ta4+ self-doped oxygen vacancy narrows the band gap of Ta2O5, optimizes the band position and creates new defect energy level. These improve the light absorption capacity to visible light and promotes the photogenerated charge transfer. The graphene as electron trap suppresses the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. The graphene as light sensitizer expands the absorption to visible light. The synergy of these multiple structure engineering realizes an excellent photocatalytic performance of Ta2O5 for solar-light driven tetracycline photodegradation. The photodegradation efficiency of 60 min solar-light irradiation is 6.6 times higher, apparent kinetic constant is 19.3 times higher and photocurrent density is 13.4 times over Ta2O5. The study also paves an avenue for construction of metal nanomaterials with desirable photoelectric properties in catalysis, energy storage and conversion and sensing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The paper reports synthesis of Ta2O5 photocatalyst by graphene quantum dot. The multiple surface engineering constructed by graphene quantum dot realizes an excellent photocatalytic performance for solar-light driven photodegradation of tetracycline.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Main techniques for removing the antibiotics in environment water include ozonation [1], adsorption [2], biodegradation [3], electrocatalysis [4] and photocatalysis [5]. Ozonation can decompose antibiotics into small molecule compounds because of strong oxidation ability of ozone. However, Ozonation is difficult to be applied in the antibiotic treatment in environment water due to its high-cost. In addition, Ozonation may also cause serious destructive impact on natural ecosystem because ozone can kill all micro-organisms during the degradation [6]. The adsorption is simple and cheap, but it cannot degrade antibiotics into small molecule compounds. Furthermore, the regeneration of adsorbent used may produce toxic wastewater, waste gas and solid waste. Biodegradation can decompose antibiotics into non-poisonous small molecules under a relatively mild condition. However, it needs the use of microorganism and natural enzyme. The change on environment factors such as pH, temperature and ionic strength may largely influence the degradation efficiency [7]. The electrocatalysis can decompose antibiotics into small molecule compounds, but it is expensive and inefficient when it was used for the antibiotic treatment in environment water. The photocatalysis has received a more attention in the degradation of antibiotics because of its low cost, no secondary pollution and mild reaction conditions [8].

Several structure engineering have been developed for improving the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [9,10]. The first structure engineering is to construct the special morphology of photocatalyst [11]. The morphology engineering can create the nanostructure with small size and pores [12] to increase the photoactive sites and exposure of special crystal plane, which can offer a better photocatalytic activity compared with other crystal planes [13]. Meng et al. reported the synthesis of monoclinic BiVO4 via hydrothermal method [14]. The investigation revealed that the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4 depends on the morphology. Because of more exposure of the (010) facet, shuriken-like BiVO4 exhibits the best photocatalytic activity among all BiVO4 samples for the photodegradation of rhodamine B. The photodegradation efficiency of 4 h visible light irradiation reaches 94.7 %. The value is more than 2.3-fold that with the BiVO4 nanorods. The second structure engineering is to dope non-metallic [15], metallic atom [16] or special structure into the photocatalyst [17–19]. The introduction of heteroatoms can optimize the band structure and results in the shift of optical response from ultraviolet light to visible spectral range. In addition, the element doping can also enhance the adsorption ability of photocatalyst to organic pollutants. Ismail et al. reported the synthesis of S-doped Ta2O5 for the photodegradation of methylene blue [20]. The S-doping achieves to 3.4 times increase in photodegradation efficiency. The third structure engineering is to introduce defect into crystal structure of photocatalyst [21]. The defect engineering narrows the band gap, optimize the band structure and creates new defect energy level. These lead to an improved light absorption capacity towards visible light [22]. However, the introduction of defects may cause the distortion of crystal structure and brings the loss of structural stability. The fourth structure engineering is to introduce carrier traps to capture the photogenerated charges. The presence of carrier traps can suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [23]. Huang et al. reported the synthesis of Cu and W co-doped TiO2 for photocatalytic oxidation of methane [24]. During the photocatalytic reaction, the oxygen vacancy acts as one hole trap to capture the photogenerated holes, and the W6+-dopant state acts as an electron trap to capture the photogenerated electrons. The presence of these carrier traps effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. As a result, the oxygen productivity reaches 34.5 mmol g−1 with the selectivity of 97.1 %. The fifth structure engineering is to introduce light sensitizer to expand the spectral range of light absorption. This improves the light absorption capacity of photocatalyst [25]. Ma et al. reported the synthesis of Zn-TCPP sensitized g-C3N4 photocatalyst by thermal polycondensation [26]. The introduction of 10 % Zn-TCPP sensitizer promotes the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. This realizes an improvement of photocatalytic activity. The resultant photocatalyst was successfully applied in photodegradation of methylene blue with the total removal rate of 80.3 %. The sixth structure engineering is to construct various heterojunction semiconductors by introducing other semiconductor or noble metal nanoparticle to fabricate more efficient charge transfer pathway [27,28]. The presence of heterojunction not only enhances the visible light absorption capacity but also accelerates the photocatalytic reaction. Guo et al. reported the construction of S-scheme charge transfer pathway by introducing CdS quantum dot into (Al, C)-Ta2O5 nanosheets [29]. The combination of Ta and C doping with the S-scheme charge transfer pathway achieves to an enhanced visible-light absorption capacity and charge separation rate. The degradation rate is 27.24 times higher over (Al, C)-Ta2O5 and 2.43-times higher over CdS quantum dot. Huang et al. fabricated one Z-scheme charge transfer pathway by combination of Ag/AgCl nanoparticles with defects-engineered Ta2O5-x. The resultant Ag/AgCl/Ta2O5-x composite exhibits a higher photocatalytic activity compared with single Ag/AgCl and single Ta2O5-x [30]. However, the actual effect of each structure engineering on improving the photocatalytic performance is very limited. To further improve the photocatalytic performance, researchers attempt to construct multiple structure engineering in a photocatalyst [31]. Chen et al. reported the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs)@Cu-8-hydroxyquinoline-5-carbox-ylic acid (HQCA)/TiO2 photocatalyst. The synthesis includes five cumulative processes, namely preparation of Cu-HQCA, TiO2, Cu-HQCA/TiO2, CDs and CDs@Cu-HQCA/TiO2 [32]. The synergistic effect of light sensitizer, morphology engineering and surface modification realizes an improved adsorption-photocatalytic performance for removal of dexamethasone. The degradation reaches to 99.4 % with a mineralization degree of 77.4 %. The result demonstrates that the construction of multiple structure engineering is one powerful tool to achieve significant enhancement in the photocatalytic performance. However, the above synthesis requires the use of complex reactions, combination of multi-step reactions, strict control of reaction conditions and long reaction time. This greatly restricts the large-scale production of photocatalyst with multiple structure engineering. To date, scalable synthesis of photocatalyst with multiple structure engineering still faces great challenges.

In the study, we reported one strategy for scalable synthesis of Ta2O5 photocatalyst by introducing glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (GGQD). The GGQD was used for construction of multiple structure engineering, including morphology engineering, surface modification, defect engineering, electron trap and light sensitizer. The synergy of these structure engineering greatly improves the photocatalytic performance of Ta2O5 for solar-light driven photodegradation of tetracycline.

Citric acid (99.9 %), glycine (Gly) (99.9 %), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) (99.8 %), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) (99.99 %), 2-isopropanol (IPA) (99.9 %), disodium ethylenediamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA) (99.9 %), tryptophan (Try) (99.8 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company. High purity tantalum pentachloride (TaCl5) in n-butanol was purchased from Hunan Huajing powdery Material Science & Technological Co., Ltd. Tantalic acid (Ta2O5·xH2O) was prepared by following procedure: 4 mL of TaCl5 in n-butanol was added into a mixed solution containing 10 mL of water and 5 mL of ethanol under stirring. Its pH value was adjusted to 9 with 1 M NaOH and then centrifugated at 3500 rpm for 5 min. The collected precipitate was washed with ethanol and ultrapure water until the supernatant reaches to neutral. Other reagents used were of analytical grade and were purchased from Sinopharm Shanghai reagent company. Deionized (DI) water (18 μS cm−1) was used throughout the experiment.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was carried out on HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was conducted on JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope at 200 keV. The TEM sample was prepared by dispersing small amount of final material powder into ethanol with ultrasound. Then, one drop of the suspension was dropped on 300 mesh copper. The TEM grid was covered with thin amorphous carbon film. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured on X-ray D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using a Cu Kα radiation source with λ = 0.15406 nm. The infrared (IR) spectrum was measured on Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurement was performed on ESCALAB 250Xi photoemission spectrometer having Al Kα (1486.6 eV) dual anode as one source operating at 14.6 kV of anode voltage and 13.5 mA filament current. The XPS data was collected with pass energy of 20 eV at 4 × l0−9 m bar vacuum. Initially the XPS unit was calibrated using Fermi edge of Ag (KE 1482.544). The C1s line of adventitious carbon with BE= 284.8 eV was taken as one reference for the surface-charging correction. After subtracting the linear-type background (or Shirley background for fitting the full Ta 4 f region), the core-level spectra were decomposed into their components with the mixed Gaussian-Lorentzian lines by one non-linear least squares curve-fitting procedure using the CasaXPS software. The binding energies and FWHM of the peaks were determined from the fitting results. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image was taken by Multimode 8 Force Microscope. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) was measured on Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus. The electron paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectrum was measured on Bruker EMX PLUS spectrometer at X-band (≈9.8 GHz) at room temperature. The fluorescence spectrum and intensity were measured on Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The UV–visible spectrum was recorded on TU-1901 spectrometer with DH-2000 deuterium and tungsten halogen light source in the absorbance mode (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was tested by CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer, and the excitation wavelength was 250 nm. The Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) measurement was conducted using EMXplus-10/12 electron spin resonance spectrometer. The high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) measurement was performed on MALDI SYNAPT MS ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometer.

GGQD was prepared using a reported method [33]. Typically, glycine (3 g) was mixed with citric acid (38 g) and then heated at 185 °C for 3.5 h. The resulting GGQD crude product was dissolved in ultrapure water to produce the GGQD aqueous solution of 160 mg mL−1. To obtain a solid GGQD, it was treated by dialysis with the dialysis bag (3500 Da) and freeze-drying.

The newly prepared tantalum acid (Ta2O5·xH2O) (0.3 g) was mixed with 30 wt% H2O2 solution (8 mL) and 160 mg mL−1 GGQD aqueous solution (2, 4, 6 or 8 mL) and then stirred magnetically at 70 °C until one transparent solution was formed. The aqueous solution was evaporated at 100 °C until all free water was removed. Followed by thermal annealing in N2 at 700 °C for 2 h with heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Basing on the volume of GGQD solution used, the as-prepared samples were termed as GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and GGQD-Ta2O5-8. The above procedure was also used for the preparation of Ta2O5 unless no addition of GGQD.

Photocatalytic performance of GGQD-Ta2O5 samples was tested by photodegradation of tetracycline in an aqueous solution. For the testing, one 300 W xenon lamp equipped with an ultraviolet cut-off filter (k > 420 nm) was used for simulating solar-light and visible light. In a typical procedure, 10 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL of tetracycline aqueous solution (20 mg L−1). Before the illumination, the above suspension was placed in a dark room and stirred for 60 min to reach an adsorption equilibrium state. To ensure that the concentration of free tetracycline at the equilibrium state reaches 20 mg L−1, an appropriate amount of tetracycline was added to the system. After that, turn on light source without or with ultraviolet cut-off filter to trigger photodegradation of tetracycline. During the degradation, at intervals of 15 min, 5 m L of suspension were extracted and then centrifuged to obtain the supernatant for detection of tetracycline. The concentration of tetracycline was measured using spectrophotometer at 554 nm. To evaluate the reusability of GGQD-Ta2O5-6, ten cycles of tetracycline degradation experiments were continuously carried out. After each degradation experiment, the used catalyst was collected by centrifugation, washing with deionized water and drying at 60 °C for the next photodegradation experiment.

One classical three-electrode testing system was used for calculating the photochemical properties of various photocatalysts on CHI660E electrochemical workstation. The platinum wire, Ag/AgCl and FTO plate modified with photocatalyst were employed for the counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode. The working electrode was prepared by following procedure: 10 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in a mixed solution composing of 1.0 mL of ethanol and 20 μL of Nafion (10 %) by ultrasound to form stable dispersion. The 100 μL of above dispersion was dropped onto the surface of clean FTO glass (1.0 × 1.0 cm). Then, it was dried for 2 h in a dry air. The light source was a 300 W xenon lamp, whereas the electrolyte was a 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The transient photocurrent measurement was used for measuring steady-state photocurrent density of photocatalyst by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in dark or 300 W xenon lamp illumination, in which the scan rate is 10 mV s−1 and potential range is in the range from − 0.6–0.4 V. The separation and migration of photogenerated electrons and holes were characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In the EIS measurement, the potential amplitude is ± 5 mV and the frequency range is 0.01–105 Hz. The Mott-Schottky curve of photocatalyst was measured at the frequency of 1000 Hz, and the flat band potential of the sample was calculated.
The photocatalytic active species analysis, ESR test and total organic carbon (TOC) test were shown in ESI.

Fig. 1 A shows the scheme for synthesis of GGQD and GGQD-Ta2O5. Firstly, GGQD was prepared by heating mixture of glycine and citric acid with a mass ratio of 3:38 at 185 °C for 3.5 h. The resulting GGQD was characterized by TEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), AFM, FTIR spectrum, XPS spectrum and UV absorption spectrum techniques (Fig. s1, Fig. s2, Fig. s3 and Fig. s4). The as-prepared GGQD is composed of 1–5 layers of quasi zero-dimensional graphene sheets. The graphene sheets offer an average size of 5.6 ± 0.27 nm and contain many functional groups such as -COOH, -OH, -NH2 and -NH-. The reduced GGQD by annealing in N2 at 700 °C for 2 h exhibits one strong absorption capacity in wide spectrum range of 200–800 nm (2. GGQD characterization in ESI).

The as-prepared GGQD was used for synthesis of GGQD-Ta2O5. Firstly, GGQD was combined with newly prepared tantalum acid (Ta2O5•xH2O) to form GGQD-Ta complex via Ta-O and Ta-N coordination bonds. Due to good water-solubility, the formed GGQD-Ta complexes can fully dissolve in water to form an homogeneous aqueous solution (Fig. s5). As a consequence, the coordination of Ta(V) with GGQD effectively prevents the hydrolysis of Ta(V) and finally leads to formation of small Ta2O5 nanorods (Fig. s6). However, classical graphene quantum dot cannot completely inhibit the hydrolysis of Ta(V) ions because it contains fewer -COOH and -OH groups than GGQD. Then, the complex was annealed in N2 atmosphere at 700 °C for 2 h to obtain GGQD-Ta2O5–6. In the process, Ta(V) ions were gradually released from the GGQD-Ta complex with breaking of Ta-O and Ta-N bonds at a high temperature and converted into Ta2O5 nanocrystals. The slow release effect of Ta(V) ions produced by GGQD helps to produce small rod-like Ta2O5 nanocrystals, because a slow release process can provide a sufficient time for growth of Ta2O5 nanocrystals, especially to the high-index facets. The decomposition of GGQD in a hypoxic environment produces substantial reducing gases [34]. These gases can reduce Ta5+ into Ta4+ and induces the production of Ta4+ self-doped oxygen vacancies [35]. However, a small amount of GGQDs were still retained during the annealing. These GGQDs were covalently connected to the surface of Ta2O5 nanocrystals, achieving to the GGQD modification on surface of Ta2O5 nanocrystals. Due to special optical and electrical properties of the reduced GGQD, the surface modification facilitates the stability of Ta2O5 nanocrystals, improves the light absorption capacity and captures the photogenerated electrons.

The as-synthesized GGQD-Ta2O5-6 was characterized by SEM, TEM, high resolution TEM (HRTEM), EDS element mapping, XRD, Raman spectrum, IR spectrum, EPR and XPS techniques. Fig. 1B-J present the SEM, TEM, enlarged TEM, HRTEM images and EDS elemental mappings of tantalum (Ta), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), carbon (C) and merged element mapping of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The GGQD-Ta2O5-6 shows a rod-like nanostructure with an average length of 87.9 ± 1.8 nm and average width of 41.5 ± 1.2 nm, which are much smaller than that of pristine Ta2O5 in Fig. s6. The reduced particle size is mainly attributed to the inhibition of Ta(V) hydrolysis and slow release process of Ta(V) ions caused by introducing GGQD. There are many small graphene sheets marked by yellow circles on the surface of Ta2O5 nanorods and surrounding region on the enlarged TEM image, verifying the existence of GGQD modification layer. There are one spacing of 0.232 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene, and one spacing of 0.342 nm on the HRTEM image, corresponding to the (100) plane of Ta2O5 nanorods. In addition, there are also many dark stripes and dots marked with yellow circles on the HRTEM image. These defects are attributed to the oxygen vacancies caused by Ta4+ self-doping. The EDS element mappings demonstrate the spatial distribution of Ta, O, N and C elements in the surface of GGQD-Ta2O5. Ta element only come from Ta2O5, N and C elements only come from GGQD, and O elements come from Ta2O5 and GGQD. Thus, the results of element mapping analysis confirm again that the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 contains Ta2O5 and GGQD.

Fig. 2 presents the XRD patterns, Raman spectra, FTIR spectrum and EPR spectra of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5. There are seventeen XRD diffraction peaks at 22.85°, 28.28°, 28.78° 36.66°, 37.07°, 46.69°, 49.75°, 50.69°, 55.48°, 56.29°, 58.49°, 63.63°, 70.59°, 71.37°, 79,97°, 80.22° and 84.58° on the XRD pattern of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The peaks at 22.85°, 28.28°, 36.66°, 37.07°, 46.69°, 49.75°, 50.69°, 55.48°, 56.29°, 58.49°, 63.63°, 70.59°, 71.37°, 79,97°, 80.22° and 84.58° could be assigned at the (001), (110), (111), (201), (370), (0181), (3110), (1112), (202), (3111), (2221), (1111), (3112), (1113), (203) and (591) faces of Ta2O5 nanocrystal (PDF No.71–0639), respectively. The weak shoulder peak at 28.78° is attributed to the structural distortion of Ta2O5 nanocrystal caused by oxygen vacancies. No characteristic XRD diffraction peak at about 26° appears on the XRD pattern of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. This is because the graphene sheets offer a poor crystallinity, leading to a reduced intensity of XRD diffraction peak at 26°. This peak is weak enough to be invisible in the XRD pattern in Fig. 2A. To facilitate the comparison, the ratios (I(f)) of the XRD peak intensity to the strongest peak intensity for Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6. Table s1 shows that the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 offers more exposed high-index facets, such as (202), (3111), (2221), (1111), (3112), ((1113), (203) and (591) facets. More exposure to high-index facets leads to an improved photocatalytic activity. There are several weak Raman peaks at 72.5, 253.3, 631.5, 709.9, 903.2, 1327.4 and 1614.7 cm−1 on the Raman spectrum of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The peaks at 72.5, 253.3, 631.5, 709.9 and 903.2 cm−1 could be assigned at the characteristic Raman peaks of Ta2O5 nanocrystals [36]. The strong Raman peaks at 1327.4 and 1614.7 cm−1 could be assigned at the characteristic D peak and G peak of graphene. There are several IR absorption peaks and absorption bands at 3413, 1617, 1400, 1084, 620 and 526 cm−1 on the FTIR spectrum of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The strong and wide absorption band between 3600 and 3000 cm−1 is the IR absorption caused by symmetric vibration of N-H and O-H bonds. The moderately strong peak at 1617 cm−1 owe to the stretching vibration of CO bond. The weak peak at 1400 cm−1 is the IR absorption of stretching vibration of C-O bond. The strong peak at 1084 cm−1 owe to the IR absorption produced by out-of-plane bending vibration of O-H bond. The results of IR analysis demonstrate that GGQD-Ta2O5 exists -OH, -COOH, and -NH- groups. Different from Ta2O5, the EPR spectrum of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 shows a symmetrical EPR signal at g = 2.002. The prominent resonance line of paramagnetic phase in the EPR absorption spectrum of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 demonstrates again that there are more Ta4+ on the surface of Ta2O5 nanorods. This further verifies the self-doping of Ta4+ in Ta2O5 nanorods results in formation of oxygen vacancies on the surface.

Fig. 3 shows the high resolution Ta4f, O1s, C s and N1s XPS spectra of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. To demonstrate the existence of oxygen vacancies on the surface of GGQD-Ta2O5-6, high resolution Ta 4 f and O 1 s XPS spectra of pristine Ta2O5 were added into Fig. 3A and B. The high resolution Ta4f XPS spectrum of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 could be divided into four peaks at 25.1, 26.4, 26.9 and 28.2 eV. The peaks at 25.1 and 26.9 eV correspond to the Ta 4f7/2 and Ta 4f5/2 of Ta4+, verifying the existence of Ta4+ specie in GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The peaks at 26.4 and 28.2 eV corresponds to the Ta 4f7/2 and Ta 4f5/2 of Ta5+ [37]. Compared with the pristine Ta2O5, the Ta 4f7/2 and Ta 4f5/2 peaks of Ta5+ for GGQD-Ta2O5-6 indicates a reduced binding energy. This confirms that there are an obvious difference in chemical environment of Ta atom in pristine Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 [38]. The high resolution O1s XPS spectrum of GGQD-Ta2O5 could be divided into two peaks at 529.3 and 531.9 eV, corresponding to the lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen in GGQD-Ta2O5-6. As the existence of water weakens the Ta-O bond, the two peaks of O1s for GGQD-Ta2O5-6 shifts from a higher binding energy to a lower binding energy. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s could be divided into five peaks at 284.6, 285.1, 286.6, 288.4 and 290.1 eV. These peaks correspond to the C-C, C-O, CO, RO-CO and C-N bonds, respectively. The high resolution N1s XPS spectrum of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 could be divided into three peaks at 399.5, 403.6 and 405.5 eV, corresponding to the pyrrolic N, amine N and graphitic N. The pyrrolic N come from the doping nitrogen at the edge of graphene sheets, the amine N comes from the amide bond formed by the condensation of amino in glycine molecule and carboxyl groups at the edge of graphene sheets, and graphitic N is the doped nitrogen at the lattice of graphene sheets.

Fig. 4 shows the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of GGQD, GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6, GGQD-Ta2O5-8 and Ta2O5, plot of the transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. the energy of light for GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6, GGQD-Ta2O5-8 and Ta2O5, Mott–Schottky plots for GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5 in 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution, and Nyquist plots of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5. The absorption of pristine Ta2O5 is mainly concentrated in ultraviolet region (Fig. 4A). Thus, it only can offer a low photocatalytic activity under solar light irradiation. Different from pristine Ta2O5, the GGQD displays a strong and continuous absorption band from ultraviolet region to visible region. The characteristic makes the GGQD modification acts as a light sensitizer to enhance the light absorption capacity of Ta2O5 to visible light. With the increase of GGQD, the absorption intensity of GGQD-Ta2O5 will rapidly increase. However, the GGQD-Ta2O5-8 shows a lower visible light absorption capacity compared with GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The result confirms that the excessive GGQDs in GGQD-Ta2O5 are not good for improving the light absorption. The band gap energies (Eg) of different semiconductor materials can be estimated by basing on the Kubelka-Munk function versus the energy of light. The obtained Eg values are 2.96 for GGQD-Ta2O5-6, 3.05 for GGQD-Ta2O5-8, 3.37 for GGQD-Ta2O5-4, 3.45 for GGQD-Ta2O5-2 and 3.72 eV for Ta2O5 (Fig. 4B). These data demonstrate that the GGQD-Ta2O5 has a narrower band gap energy compared with pristine Ta2O5. Among all GGQD-Ta2O5 samples, the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 gives the narrowest band gap energy. The reduced band gap energy is attributed to the production of Ta4+ self-doped oxygen vacancies. The defect engineering narrows the band gap energy and leads to an enhanced light absorption capacity. The Mott-Schottky plots of both Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 show one positive slope (Fig. 4C), verifying that Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 are n-type semiconductors. The conduction band potential (ECB) can be obtained by tangents of Mott-Schottky plots for Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 extension with the abscissa axis. The results show that the ECB values are − 0.68 V vs. Ag/AgCl for Ta2O5 (−0.46 V vs. SHE) and − 0.87 V vs. Ag/AgCl for GGQD-Ta2O5-6 (−0.65 V vs. SHE). Further, the valence band potentials (EVB) were calculated by using the following formula: EVB (V vs. SHE) =Eg +ECB. The obtained EVB values are 3.26 V for Ta2O5 and 2.31 V for GGQD-Ta2O5-6. Fig. 4D presents the Nyquist plots of Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 in 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. It can be seen from Fig. 4D that the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 indicates a significantly reduced charge transfer resistance compared with Ta2O5. The result demonstrates that the presence of GGQD modification can promotes the interfacial charge transfer. As a result, the GGQD modification layer acts as an electron trapper to suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs during the photocatalytic reaction process [39].

The fluorescence intensity can reflect the recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes. A lower fluorescence intensity presents a slower recombination process of photogenerated carriers [40]. To study on the effect of GGQD on the separation of photogenerated carriers in Ta2O5, the photoluminescence spectra of different materials were measured under the excitation of 335 nm ultraviolet. Fig. 4E and F show the fluorescence emission spectra of Ta2O5, GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and GGQD-Ta2O5-8. Among all photocatalysts, Ta2O5 shows the strongest fluorescence intensity, verifying that Ta2O5 has the fastest recombination process of photogenerated electrons and holes. However, the introduction of GGQD results in a reduced fluorescence intensity. With the increase of GGQD the fluorescence intensity firstly decrease and then decreases. This is because the reduced GGQD has a high conductivity. The reduced GGQD can act as the electron trap to rapidly capture the photogenerated electrons. This effectively suppresses the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Among all photocatalysts, the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 displays the lowest fluorescence intensity. The result demonstrates that the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 gives the fastest separation process of photogenerated carriers. Further, the photoelectrochemical H2 generation in dark or 300 W xenon lamp illumination was used to examine the photocatalytic activities of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. Fig. 4F presents the linear sweep voltammogram plots of Ta2O5 and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 in 300 W xenon lamp illumination. GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5 indicate a negligible dark current. This demonstrates that the photoelectrochemical H2 generation cannot occur in dark. However, the light illumination brings an obviously increase in photocurrent. The photocurrent density of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 reaches 0.336 mA cm−2 under the illumination, which is more than 13.4-fold that of the pristine Ta2O5 (0.025 mA cm−2). The increased photocurrent density demonstrates that the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 has a much higher photocatalytic activity compared with the pristine Ta2O5.

Tetracycline was selected as one refractory antibiotic to evaluate the adsorption-photodegradation performance of different photocatalyst under solar-light irradiation. Since the intimate contact of photocatalyst with tetracycline can ensure the redox of tetracycline on the surface of photocatalyst, a good adsorption may bring a high photodegradation rate. Thus, the adsorption capacity of photocatalyst to tetracycline become an important factor affecting the photodegradation rate. To evaluate the adsorption ability of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 towards tetracycline, the adsorption efficiency-time plots for tetracycline with different photocatalysts in dark were measured, respectively. Fig. s7 shows for all plots the adsorption efficiency rapidly increases with the increase of degradation time when the time is less than 15 min. The growth rate will slow down when the time is more than 15 min and then reaches to the maximum at the time of 30 min, indicating an adsorption equilibrium state. Further study reveals that within 30 min from the beginning of adsorption the logarithm value of adsorption efficiency has a good linear relationship with time, conforming to the kinetic characteristics of first-order reaction. Thus, the adsorption mainly is one physical process. However, the different photocatalyst shows a different adsorption behavior. Ta2O5 has the lowest adsorption capacity, because of small surface area of Ta2O5 and weak interaction between Ta2O5 and tetracycline. Different from Ta2O5, GGQD displays the best adsorption capacity. This is attributed to unique structure of GGQD. On the one hand, GGQD has the rich of active sites for adsorbing tetracycline because of its large specific surface. On the other hand, GGQD can strongly bind tetracycline molecules by π-π stacking between graphene sheets in GGQD and the aromatic ring in tetracycline, Van der waals force and hydrogen bond between different functional groups in GGQD and tetracycline. The strong interaction make tetracycline can be firmly adsorbed on the graphene sheets of GGQD. The combination of two factors result in an enhanced adsorption capacity of GGQD towards tetracycline. As a result, the introduction of GGQD as an adsorbent significantly improves the absorption efficiency of Ta2O5 to tetracycline. A high content of GGQD in GGQD-Ta2O5 brings a higher absorption efficiency. The suggest has been well demonstrated by the results of adsorption efficiency measurements in Fig. s7.

Fig. 5A and B show the solar-light driven photodegradation efficiencies of tetracycline with different photocatalysts and first-order kinetic curves. In the absence of photocatalyst, the solar-light radiation cannot cause an obvious change in the concentration of tetracycline. This verifies that the self-degradation of tetracycline can be ignored. In the presence of GGQD, the solar-light irradiation only can cause a slight drop in the concentration of tetracycline with the increase of time. This demonstrates that the GGQD can catalyze the photodegradation of tetracycline, but the photocatalytic activity is extremely low. Compared with GGQD, Ta2O5 displays a better photodegradation efficiency. This is because Ta2O5 can absorb the ultraviolet light in solar-light and shows a relatively high photocatalytic activity. However, the photodegradation efficiency is significantly lower than that with GGQD-Ta2O5 samples. The degradation efficiencies of 60 min of solar-light radiation are 67.9 % of GGQD-Ta2O5-2, 91.1% of GGQD-Ta2O5-4, 94.6 % of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 % and 81.7 % of GGQD-Ta2O5-8 from 14.3 % of Ta2O5 and 2.1 % of GGQD. The above data demonstrate that GGQD-Ta2O5-6 exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity for degradation of tetracycline. The degradation efficiency is more than 6.6-fold that of Ta2O5 and 45-fold that of GGQD. Further, the kinetic behavior of photodegradation process with different photocatalysts was investigated. Fig. 5B shows the first-order kinetic curves with different photocatalysts. The apparent kinetic constant value (k) of photocatalytic reaction was obtained by calculating the slope of first-order kinetic curve. The k value with GGQD-Ta2O5-6 is up to 0.0503 min−1, which is more than 19.3-fold that with Ta2O5.

Fig. 5C shows the visible light driven photodegradation efficiencies of tetracycline with different photocatalysts. The photodegradation efficiency with GGQD is close to zero. The result demonstrates that the GGQD cannot catalyze the degradation of tetracycline under visible light radiation. The photodegradation efficiency with Ta2O5 is less than 0.4%, which is much lower than that under solar-light radiation (14.3 %). This owes to wide band gap of Ta2O5 (3.72 eV). Such a wide band gap makes Ta2O5 can only absorb ultraviolet light but visible light in solar-light. As a result, the Ta2O5 almost cannot catalyze the degradation of tetracycline under visible light irradiation. However, GGQD-Ta2O5-2, GGQD-Ta2O5-4, GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and GGQD-Ta2O5-8 still keep a high photocatalytic activity. Their photodegradation efficiencies of 60 min visible light irradiation are up to 40.7% of GGQD-Ta2O5-2, 79.7 % of GGQD-Ta2O5-4, 91.8 % of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 % and 69.5 % of GGQD-Ta2O5-8. Among four kinds of GGQD-Ta2O5 samples, the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 exhibits the highest photocatalytic activity. The degradation efficiency is more than 229.5-fold that with Ta2O5. The data demonstrate that the introduction of GGQD can conspicuously improve the visible light absorption capacity of Ta2O5. Further, the kinetic behaviors of different photocatalyst in the photodegradation process were investigated under visible light irradiation. Fig. 5D and E present the first-order kinetic curves and corresponding k values. The k value of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 reaches 0.0415 min−1, which is more than 138.3-fold that of Ta2O5.

Fig. 5 F shows the k values of photodegradation reactions with different photocatalysts under solar-light irradiation and visible light irradiation were shown in. The GGQD-Ta2O5-6 gives the highest photocatalytic activity under the irradiation of both solar-light and visible light. The difference (Δk) between the K value under solar light and the k value under visible light mainly presents the photocatalytic activity under the ultraviolet light. The Δk with GGQD-Ta2O5-6 is about 0.0088 min−1, which is more 3-fold that of Ta2O5 (0.0023 min−1). Basing on the above results, one conclusions can be drawn: the introduction of GGQD can improve the absorption capacity of both ultraviolet and visible light, but the improvement of visible light absorption capacity is more significant. Consequently, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 is mainly attributed to its full use of visible light in solar-light.

To test the practicability of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 in the photodegradation of tetracycline, the effects of catalyst dosage, tetracycline concentration and medium pH on the photodegradation efficiency were investigated under solar-light radiation. Fig. s8 shows the degradation efficiency of 60 min solar light irradiation in the different dosage of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. In the absence of GGQD-Ta2O5-6, the degradation efficiency is close to zero. This demonstrates that the self-degradation of tetracycline can be ignored. With the increase of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 dosage, the degradation efficiency will rapidly increase when the dosage is less than 7.5 mg. However, the degradation efficiency tends to stable when the dosage is more than 10 mg. To obtain a high degradation efficiency and save the catalyst, 10 mg of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 was used for the tetracycline photodegradation.

Fig. 6A presents the solar-light driven photodegradation efficiencies of tetracycline with GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The degradation rate of 60 min solar-light radiation are 96.7 % of 10 mg L−1 tetracycline, 94.8 % of 20 mg L−1 tetracycline, 88.4 % of 30 mg L−1 tetracycline, 74.5 % of 40 mg L−1 tetracycline and 59.8 % of 50 mg L−1 tetracycline. In general, the degradation rate will decrease with the increase of tetracycline concentration. This is because the degradation rate highly depends on the number of photoactive sites provided by the photocatalyst. In the presence of a high concentration of tetracycline, the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 cannot provide enough photoactive sites for the photodegradation reaction. This leads to a gradual decrease in photodegradation rate of tetracycline. However, the degradation rate still remains > 90 % when a relatively high tetracycline concentration of 10 and 20 mg L−1 was used. This is because GGQD-Ta2O5-6 has the rich of active sites and strong adsorption capacity to tetracycline. This creates a good condition for the intimate contact of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 with tetracycline molecule to carry out the photocatalytic reaction. This achieves to a high degradation rate. Fig. 6B presents the solar-light driven photodegradation efficiencies of tetracycline in different pH values with GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The degradation efficiencies of 60 min solar-light irradiation are 90.6 % of pH 3, 94.8 % of pH 4.3, 81.8 % of pH 7, 71.3 % of pH 9 % and 63.2 % of pH 11. The data show that GGQD-Ta2O5-6 in a slightly acidic environment offers a better photocatalytic activity compared with in a more alkaline environment. In an alkaline environment, many tetracycline hydrochloride molecules and GGQD-Ta2O5-6 are changed into the negatively charged species due to the dissociation of chemically active groups. The strong repulsive force between GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and tetracycline makes them away from each other and leads to a reduced photocatalytic activity. In addition, the existence of negative charges on the surface of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 will weaken or even lose the ability of GGQD to capture the photogenerated electrons. This further slows down the separation of charge carries and results in a reduced photocatalytic activity. However, in neutral or slightly acidic environment the most GGQD-Ta2O5–6 and tetracycline hydrochloride molecules can keep neutral. This is conducive to maintain a higher photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of tetracycline and realizes a higher degradation efficiency compared with in a more alkaline environment.

To evaluate the reusability of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 photocatalyst, ten consecutive cyclic experiments were carried out under the solar-light irradiation by using the same procedure and reaction conditions. Fig. 6C and D present solar-light driven photodegradation rates of tetracycline with GGQD-Ta2O5-6. The ten consecutive cyclic experiments only cause a slight change in photocatalytic activity. The degradation rate of 60 min solar-light irradiation still remains 94.2% after ten consecutive cyclic experiments. The value is almost equivalent to the degradation efficiency of first experiment (94.7 %), verifying that GGQD-Ta2O5-6 offers a good reusability. Further, the morphology and crystal structure of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 after ten cycles were characterized by SEM and XPS techniques. The results were shown in and Fig. s9. The results of SEM and XPS analysis reveal that ten times of reuse did not bring an obvious change on the morphology and crystal structure of GGQD-Ta2O5-6. This demonstrates that GGQD-Ta2O5-6 has a high structural stability.

The mineralization ability of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 photocatalyst to tetracycline was evaluated by TOC test. The GGQD-Ta2O5-6 exhibits an optimal mineralization ability. The mineralization rate can reach 84.7 % after 60 min of solar light illumination, while the pristine Ta2O5 is only 17.2 %.

For ease of comparison, the photocatalytic performance of different photocatalysts for photodegradation of tetracycline was shown in Table 1. Table 1 shows that the GGQD-Ta2O5-6 gives advantage of degradation rate and mineralization. Such a good photocatalytic performance of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 for the photodegradation of tetracycline under solar-light radiation is attributed to introduction of GGQD. The coordination of GGQD with Ta(V) ions to form water-soluble GGQD-Ta complex can effectively prevent the hydrolysis of Ta(V). This finally leads to formation of the Ta2O5 nanorods with small size, homogeneous morphology, high crystallinity and more exposed high-index facets. The morphology engineering increases the photoactive sites and results in an enhanced photocatalytic activity. The reduction of Ta(V) caused by GGQD induces the production of Ta4+ self-doped oxygen vacancies. The defect engineering narrows the band gap energy, improving the visible light absorption capacity, optimizes the band structure, producing more highly photoactive •O2 - and •OH free radicals, generates more efficient charge transfer pathway, accelerating photogenerated charge transfer process, and forms new defect energy level between valence band and conduction band, further improving the charge transfer efficiency. The GGQDs were reduced during the thermal annealing to produce small graphene sheets, which were covalently attached to the surface of Ta2O5 nanorods. On the one hand, the presence of graphene sheets improves the structural stability of Ta2O5 nanorods and adsorption ability towards tetracycline. The high adsorption capacity ensures that the photodegradation reaction can be carried out on the photocatalyst surface. On the other hand, the presence of conductive graphene sheets as an electron trap can rapidly capture the photogenerated electrons. This effectively suppress the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the presence of graphene sheets as the light sensitizer further expands the light absorption from ultraviolet light to visible light region. More importantly, the synergistic effect of above multiple structure engineering achieves to an excellent photocatalytic performance of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 for photodegradation of tetracycline under solar-light irradiation.

To explore the main active species in the photodegradation of tetracycline with GGQD-Ta2O5-6 under solar-light radiation, isopropanol (IPA), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA), tryptophan (TRP) and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) were used as the quenchers to capture •OH, h+, 1O2 and •O2 -, respectively. Fig. 7A presents the degradation rates in the absence and the presence of quenchers. In the absence of quencher, the degradation rate reaches 94.81 %, indicating a good photocatalytic activity. The degradation rate still remains 85.61 % after 1 mM TRP was added. The result shows the presence of TRP only brings a 8.6 % reduce in the degradation efficiency. This verifies that 1O2 is not one main active specie in the photodegradation of tetracycline. However, the addition of BQ, IPA or EDTA results in a significantly decreased degradation rate. The presence of 1 mM BQ, IPA or EDTA causes 73.8 %, 66.86 % or 42.67 % reduce in the degradation rate, indicating that •OH, h+ and •O2 - are main active species in the photodegradation of tetracycline. Further, the active species in photodegradation reaction were detected by ESR technique. No characteristic peaks of •OH, •O2 - and 1O2 can be observed from the ESR spectra in Fig. 7B-D under the dark conditions. Under visible light irradiation, one strong 1:2:2:1 characteristic peak of •OH appears in Fig. 7B and one strong 1:1:1:1 characteristic peak of •O2 - appears in Fig. 7C. These verify that the photocatalytic process can produce a large number of •OH and •O2 - free radicals. No characteristic peak of 1O2 appears in Fig. 7D, indicating that a large amount of 1O2 cannot be produced. This demonstrates again that 1O2 is not main active specie.

Basing on the results of photochemical properties and active species investigations, the energy band structures of GGQD-Ta2O5-6 and Ta2O5 and charge transfer mechanism of the photogenerated carriers in GGQD-Ta2O5 under solar-light radiation were suggested at Fig. 8. Fig. 8 shows the pristine Ta2O5 has a more proper band structure compared with TiO2. The ECB potential (−0.46 eV) is more negative than the potential of O2/•O2 - and EVB potential (3.26 eV) is more positive than potential of •OH/OH-. Thus, Ta2O5 can catalyze the reduction of O2 in air into •O2 - and oxidation of OH- into •O2 -. The production of highly active •O2 -, •OH and photogenerated hole (h+) promotes the photodegradation of tetracycline, leading to improved photocatalytic activity and mineralization level. However, a wide band gap of 3.72 eV makes the pristine Ta2O5 can also absorb ultraviolet light but not visible light. As the energy of ultraviolet light is a small part of solar-light energy, the pristine Ta2O5 only offers a poor photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of tetracycline under solar-light radiation.

To explore the photodegradation mechanism of tetracycline, HPLC-MS was used to monitor the intermediate species produced in the photocatalytic reaction process. Before the photocatalytic reaction, the HPLC chromatogram only has one peak and the result of its MS analysis was shown in Fig. s10. Fig. s10 shows one strong peak at m/z of 445 and several weak peaks. The strong peak at m/z of 445 corresponds the molecular ion of tetracycline, and the weak peaks are the fragment peaks generated by chemical bond breaking. After the photocatalytic reaction, many new peaks appear in the HPLC chromatogram, verifying the tetracycline can be degraded in the photocatalytic process. The MS analysis results of intermediate species were shown in Fig. s11-20. The sixteen kinds of main degradation products with m/z of 461 (M2), 475 (M3), 389 (M4), 279 (M5), 194 (M6), 417 (M7), 362 (M8), 272 (M9), 114 (M10), 427 (M11), 410 (M12), 340 (M13), 301 (M14), 165 (M15), 58 (M16) and 100 (M17) were discovered. Basing on the results of HPLC-MS analysis and literatures [6,9,42,43], three possible degradation pathways are suggested in Fig. 9. The pathway I is firstly hydroxylation of tetracycline to form two intermediates (m/z 461 and 475) by the addition reaction of •OH. These intermediate species are further degraded into small intermediates (m/z 389, 279, 194 and 58) by demethylation, deamination and ring-opening reaction, and finally deeply mineralized into CO2 and H2O under the synergy of •OH, •O2 - and h+. The pathway II is firstly the demethylation of tetracycline to form an intermediate (m/z 417). The intermediate was further degraded to form four degradation products (m/z: 362, 272, 114 and 100), and finally deeply mineralized into CO2 and H2O under the synergy of •OH, •O2 - and h+. The pathway III is firstly the dehydration of tetracycline to form one intermediate (m/z: 427), which was further degraded into the intermediates (m/z 410, 340, 301 165 and 100) through the deamination, demethylation and ring-opening reactions. Finally, these small intermediates were deeply mineralized into CO2 and H2O under the synergy of •OH, •O2 - and h+.

The study demonstrated the synthesis of Ta2O5 photocatalyst by introducing glycine-functionalized graphene quantum dot. The graphene quantum dot offers many functional groups such as -COOH, -OH and -NH- groups at the edge of graphene sheets, strong adsorption ability to tetracycline and high light absorption ability to ultraviolet and visible light. The graphene quantum dot can combine with Ta(V) ions to form water-soluble complex in the presence of H2O2 via Ta-O and Ta-N bonds. The combination effectively prevents the hydrolysis of Ta(V) ions and finally leads to formation of small Ta2O5 nanorods. The graphene quantum dot can partly reduce Ta(V) to Ta4+ during the thermal annealing and leads to construction of oxygen vacancies. The presence of oxygen vacancies narrows the bandgap energy and leads to an improved light absorption ability. The graphene sheets produced by the reduction of graphene quantum dots under high temperature were firmly immobilized on the surface of Ta2O5 nanocrystals. The presence of conductive graphene sheets significantly improves the adsorption ability to tetracycline, promotes the separation of photogenerated electron and holes and enhances the light absorption capacity to ultraviolet and visible light. Consequently, the introduction of graphene quantum dot brings a significantly improved photocatalytic performance of Ta2O5 for degradation tetracycline under solar light irradiation. The study also paves an avenue for synthesis of transition metal nanomaterials with desirable photoelectric properties in catalysis, energy storage and conversion and sensing.

Li Ruiyi: Investigation, review and editing; Wen Tao: Investigation and writing; Sun Xiulan: Investigation and review; Shen Yirui: Investigation; Li Zaijun: Conceptualization and methodology; Yang Yongqiang: Investigation.
